title: Der süße Brei
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 2, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 103. Text from Wikisource („Der süße Brei (1857)“, twice proofread against the original). Fresh translation made directly from the German; 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: The one hundred and third tale (KHM 103), a short cautionary fable. A pious poor girl is given a little pot that cooks sweet porridge at a word and stops at another; when her mother forgets the stopping word, the porridge floods the whole town. Older 1857 forms such as gieng, Armuth and Noth appear.

sentences.t: Es war einmal ein armes frommes Mädchen, das lebte mit seiner Mutter allein, und sie hatten nichts mehr zu essen.
sentences.l: There was once a poor pious girl, who lived with her mother alone, and they had nothing more to eat.
sentences.i: There was once a poor, pious girl who lived alone with her mother, and they had nothing left to eat.
sentences.n: The classic fairy-tale opening “Es war einmal” = “Once upon a time.” fromm = pious, devout. As often in these tales, the girl is grammatically neuter (das Mädchen), so she is later pointed to with das/es/ihm/sein (“it,” “its”); English naturally says “she/her.” The demonstrative “das lebte” resumes Mädchen: German often points back to a noun with a der/die/das.

sentences.t: Da gieng das Kind hinaus in den Wald, und begegnete ihm da eine alte Frau, die wußte seinen Jammer schon und schenkte ihm ein Töpfchen, zu dem sollt es sagen „Töpfchen, koche,“ so kochte es guten süßen Hirsenbrei, und wenn es sagte „Töpfchen, steh,“ so hörte es wieder auf zu kochen.
sentences.l: Then the child went out into the forest, and there an old woman met it, who knew its misery already and gave it a little-pot, to which it should say “Little-pot, cook,” then it cooked good sweet millet-porridge, and when it said “Little-pot, stand,” then it stopped cooking again.
sentences.i: Then the child went out into the forest, and there an old woman came to meet her, who already knew her sorrow and gave her a little pot: to it she was to say “Little pot, cook,” and it would cook good sweet millet porridge, and when she said “Little pot, stop,” it would leave off cooking again.
sentences.n: The magic words of the tale: “Töpfchen, koche” (Little pot, cook!) and “Töpfchen, steh” (Little pot, stand still! — i.e. stop!), with koche and steh as imperatives; steh is the imperative of stehen (to stand → stand still, stop). Töpfchen is the diminutive of Topf (pot). gieng = 1857 spelling of ging (went). begegnen takes the dative: “begegnete ihm” = met it (to it). sollt is a clipped sollte (should, was to). “hörte auf zu kochen” — aufhören (to stop) is separable.

sentences.t: Das Mädchen brachte den Topf seiner Mutter heim, und nun waren sie ihrer Armuth und ihres Hungers ledig und aßen süßen Brei so oft sie wollten.
sentences.l: The girl brought the pot home to her mother, and now they were free of their poverty and their hunger and ate sweet porridge as often as they wanted.
sentences.i: The girl brought the pot home to her mother, and now they were rid of their poverty and their hunger and ate sweet porridge as often as they pleased.
sentences.n: ledig (free, rid of) governs the genitive: “ihrer Armuth und ihres Hungers” = of their poverty and their hunger. heimbringen (to bring home) is separable: “brachte … heim.” Armuth is the 1857 spelling of modern Armut.

sentences.t: Auf eine Zeit war das Mädchen ausgegangen, da sprach die Mutter „Töpfchen, koche,“ da kocht es, und sie ißt sich satt; nun will sie daß das Töpfchen wieder aufhören soll, aber sie weiß das Wort nicht.
sentences.l: At one time the girl had gone out, then the mother spoke “Little-pot, cook,” then it cooks, and she eats herself full; now she wants that the little-pot should stop again, but she does not know the word.
sentences.i: One time the girl had gone out, and the mother said “Little pot, cook,” and it cooked, and she ate her fill; now she wants the little pot to stop again, but she does not know the word.
sentences.n: “Auf eine Zeit” = one time, on one occasion (an older turn of phrase). The story slips into the vivid present here (kocht, ißt, will, weiß) for immediacy, even though it began in the past. “sich satt essen” = to eat one’s fill. daß = 1857 spelling of dass.

sentences.t: Also kocht es fort, und der Brei steigt über den Rand hinaus und kocht immer zu, die Küche und das ganze Haus voll, und das zweite Haus und dann die Straße, als wollts die ganze Welt satt machen, und ist die größte Noth, und kein Mensch weiß sich da zu helfen.
sentences.l: So it cooks on, and the porridge rises out over the rim and keeps ever cooking, the kitchen and the whole house full, and the second house and then the street, as if it wanted to make the whole world full, and there is the greatest distress, and no person knows how to help himself there.
sentences.i: So it kept on cooking, and the porridge rose up over the rim and cooked on and on, filling the kitchen and the whole house, and the second house and then the street, as if it meant to feed the whole world, and there was the greatest distress, and nobody knew what to do.
sentences.n: Two ways of saying “keep on cooking”: “kocht … fort” (fortkochen, cook away) and “kocht immer zu” (zukochen, cook on and on) — both separable. “als wollts” = als wollte es (as if it wanted), with wollts a clipped contraction. “sich zu helfen wissen” = to know how to cope, what to do; “kein Mensch weiß sich da zu helfen” = nobody knows what to do. Noth = 1857 spelling of Not (distress).

sentences.t: Endlich, wie nur noch ein einziges Haus übrig ist, da kommt das Kind heim, und spricht nur „Töpfchen, steh,“ da steht es und hört auf zu kochen; und wer wieder in die Stadt wollte, der mußte sich durchessen.
sentences.l: Finally, when only a single house is still left over, then the child comes home, and speaks only “Little-pot, stand,” then it stands and stops cooking; and whoever wanted to get back into the town, he had to eat his way through.
sentences.i: At last, when only a single house is left, the child comes home and just says “Little pot, stop,” and it stands still and leaves off cooking; and whoever wanted to get back into the town had to eat his way through.
sentences.n: “wie” here is temporal = when, as. The tale ends on a wry image: “sich durchessen” (durchessen, to eat one’s way through) — anyone wanting back into town had to eat a path through the porridge. mußte = 1857 spelling of musste (had to).
